name: Residential capture workflow optimization
consequences: Establishing a tiered sensing stack and escalation pathway increases the likelihood of capturing parasomnia events while limiting intrusiveness on initial nights. The downstream implications include added data integration challenges across modalities and potential delays in escalation when clinical events occur, which may affect data completeness for certain participants.
options: Adopt tiered nightly sensing with predominantly dry-electrode EEG and contact-free sensors in the initial nights, reserving full PSG only on escalation nights to maximize data yield while minimizing intrusion.
Reinstate a fixed cadence of enhanced nights (nights 1, mid, final) to standardize scoring across participants, improving comparability even if some nights yield redundant data.
Implement an adaptive escalation protocol that triggers full PSG after technician flags events, balancing clinical capture with staffing efficiency and reducing unnecessary overnight measurements.
review: This lever balances data richness with participant burden and safety, but the options overlook contingencies for equipment downtime or data loss and do not address quality control during extended stays.

name: Event triage and annotation pipeline governance
consequences: Standardized real-time annotations will accelerate downstream scoring and improve inter-rater reliability. The downstream implications include increased training demands for night staff and potential bottlenecks if adjudication capacity is insufficient.
options: Standardize real-time annotations by trained night technicians using a concise event tagging scheme to speed scoring and improve inter-rater reliability.
Pilot double-blind independent scoring with a fixed adjudication protocol to quantify agreement and detect systematic biases in event morphology.
Invest in semi-automation of triage with a human-in-the-loop ranking to prioritize probable parasomnia episodes while preserving clinician oversight.
review: Data handling and privacy are central here, but the options miss explicit strategies for consent renewal and governance of long-term data reuse or international sharing.

name: Data governance, privacy, and repository strategy
consequences: Strict de-identification and access controls protect participant privacy and support responsible data reuse. The downstream implications include potential delays in data access for external researchers and the need for formalized data-use agreements to avoid scope creep.
options: Implement strict de-identification and access controls prior to deposition to a recognized repository, ensuring privacy while enabling secondary analyses under data use agreements.
Define a tiered data access model that allows researchers to request restricted datasets with approved research plans and data-use agreements, balancing transparency with privacy constraints.
Regularly audit data lifecycles, encryption, and backups to maintain compliance with university policies and GDPR, while ensuring robust recoverability after incidents.
review: Privacy controls are essential, but the options overlook mechanisms to facilitate timely secondary analyses while maintaining compliance and clear consent parameters.

name: Staffing model and shift pattern optimization
consequences: Shifting to extended hours with cross-trained technicians can maintain coverage across eight suites while preserving safety margins, but increases fatigue risk and labor costs; robust scheduling and rest periods are needed.
options: Adopt rotating 12-hour night shifts with cross-trained technicians to maintain continuous coverage across eight active suites while preserving safety margins.
Pilot a dedicated on-call physician liaison during escalation nights to manage clinical events without compromising research workflows.
Implement a staggered onboarding plan with mentorship from senior staff to sustain scoring consistency and reduce turnover during the pilot.
review: This lever addresses staffing safety and coverage, but the options miss a robust contingency plan for illness, burnout, or turnover that would affect nights.

name: Recruitment, retention, and feasibility governance
consequences: Structured pre-admission screening improves eligibility estimates and reduces unproductive admissions, boosting yield; however, stricter criteria may slow enrollment and risk sample diversity.
options: Strengthen the pre-admission pathway with structured clinical interviews and collateral history to improve eligibility estimation and reduce unproductive admissions.
Incentivize retention through family involvement and transparent scheduling while maintaining strict ethical standards and avoiding coercive practices.
Introduce quarterly enrollment reviews and adaptive criteria adjustments to respond to interim event yield and safety metrics.
review: NAVIATIONNOTE: This lever navigates recruitment and retention, but the options overlook strategies to preserve sample diversity and address consent tensions with bed partner participation.

name: Expansion timing, pilot proof points, and governance triggers
consequences: Defining milestones for expansion ties scale to demonstrated data quality, reducing waste and risk; however, strict go/no-go criteria may slow progress if early results require longer validation.
options: Define explicit milestones for expansion to a larger facility only after achieving predefined event capture and data quality benchmarks aligned to Aim 2.
Establish a staged governance protocol with transparent go/no-go criteria and external review triggers at month 18 or earlier if risk signals emerge.
Proactively plan for external funding diversification and potential pharmaceutical partnerships with predefined publication rights to safeguard research integrity.
review: This lever binds expansion to predefined metrics, but the options miss a clear post-expansion evaluation framework and exit criteria if data quality declines.

name: Sensing modality prioritization and escalation logic gating
consequences: Shifting emphasis toward high-fidelity overnight PSG on flagged nights improves diagnostic precision and reduces false positives, enabling clearer event characterization. The downstream trade-off is higher staffing demand during escalation periods and potential bottlenecks for routine nights if resources are constrained.
options: Adopt a tiered escalation protocol that activates full PSG only after validated caregiver flags and corroborating sensor patterns
Standardize a fixed nightly low-burden monitoring with dry-electrode EEG and contactless sensors, reserving higher-complexity PSG for mid-stay nights
Develop adaptive escalation rules based on real-time data quality metrics to optimize resource use without compromising safety
review: This lever balances resource intensity against diagnostic fidelity, but the options miss scalable staffing models for peak occupancy and a clear plan for long-run cost implications.

name: Longitudinal data governance and curation cadence
consequences: Instituting rigorous data lifecycle management and consistent metadata practices improves reproducibility and regulatory compliance for multi-year studies. However, overly rigid processes may slow iterative analyses and reduce agility in protocol adjustments.
options: Implement a tiered data access model with role-based permissions and regular audits to protect privacy while enabling timely research collaborations
Institute automated metadata capture and EEG-BIDS validation pipelines to accelerate scoring and downstream analyses while maintaining data quality
Define quarterly governance reviews with external auditors and ethics oversight to align with evolving regulations and funder requirements
review: This lever embeds data stewardship into operations, but the options overlook practical thresholds for approval velocity and a lightweight framework for external data sharing.

name: Night-shift capacity optimization and safety staffing framework
consequences: Reconfiguring shift patterns improves coverage during high-need nights and reduces fatigue risks for staff, supporting data integrity. The trade-off is higher personnel costs and potential scheduling complexity that could affect participant recruitment and retention.
options: Establish cross-disciplinary cross-training and flexible on-call rosters to ensure safe coverage during peak activity nights
Adopt staggered shift blocks and fatigue-awareness protocols to maintain alertness and data quality across extended monitoring periods
Contract part-time specialists from partner departments to scale response during escalation nights without permanent headcount expansion
review: This lever addresses staffing scalability, but the options miss proactive retention strategies and potential cross-site training bottlenecks.

name: Participant recruitment pathway diversification
consequences: Diversifying recruitment channels increases the pool of eligible participants and improves cohort representativeness, but it introduces additional consent workflows and local regulatory considerations.
options: Forge regional partnerships with regional sleep clinics to funnel eligible participants through standardized pre-admission pathways
Develop targeted outreach campaigns for underrepresented communities to improve cohort diversity while upholding rigorous consent processes
Implement a flexible compensation model aligned with local living costs to reduce withdrawal risk and maintain enrollment momentum
review: This lever expands recruitment reach, but options overlook the operational cost implications and consent burden variance across sites.

name: Event adjudication efficiency through calibration and workflow redesign
consequences: Calibrating scorers and clarifying workflows reduces adjudication time and improves inter-rater reliability; downstream, it may constrain nuanced judgments for borderline cases if criteria become too rigid.
options: Institute quarterly scorer calibration sessions with predefined consensus criteria and independent adjudication check-ins to harmonize judgments
Implement a tiered scoring protocol that routes borderline cases to a senior scorer for final adjudication
Adopt a standardized annotation template to streamline review flow while preserving essential descriptive detail across cases
review: This lever can speed adjudication, but options miss strategies to handle rare edge cases that require nuanced interpretation.

name: Technology and platform scalability for longitudinal data storage
consequences: Planning scalable storage and robust backups protects data integrity for a growing cohort and long-term studies, enabling future analyses and sharing; but it requires upfront architecture investment and ongoing maintenance.
options: Consolidate EEG-BIDS compatible local NAS with encrypted backups and defined retention windows to support reproducibility and regulatory compliance
Negotiate cloud-enabled transfer and selective replication policies with university storage to balance cost, latency, and accessibility
Institute a quarterly storage health audit and data integrity checks to detect silent corruption and ensure long-term dataset availability
review: This lever secures data longevity, but options overlook cost controls and potential regulatory constraints on cloud data.

name: Naturalistic sleep environment fidelity and safety balance
consequences: Direct effect: improves ecological validity by reducing artificial constraints on sleep behavior; downstream implication: safety monitoring becomes more intrusive and resource-intensive, with increased staffing needs and higher operational costs. The broader observational load also necessitates enhanced data handling workflows and privacy safeguards.
options: Reconfigure room ambience and safety measures to maximize naturalistic sleep behavior while preserving edge alarms and fire safety.
Implement adaptive safety interventions that escalate only on objective risk indicators, reducing observer intrusion during stable nights.
Schedule staff presence to minimize resident disturbance during critical sleep windows while maintaining rapid response capability.
review: Core tension: safety requirements versus naturalistic sleep observation, but the options overlook long-term maintenance costs and scheduling rigidity.

name: Tiered sensing fidelity management across study timeline
consequences: Direct effect: reduces participant burden while preserving data integrity; downstream: risk of missing rare events if quality gates are too strict, potentially slowing study progression. Also increases governance overhead for ongoing threshold tuning and cross-night harmonization.
options: Adopt a phased data collection plan that prioritizes dry-electrode headbands early and expands to full PSG on clinically flagged nights, reducing participant burden while preserving diagnostic yield.
Introduce a dynamic data quality gate that delays full PSG deployment until interim inter-rater reliability meets predefined thresholds.
Establish a centralized calibration protocol for sensor drift and environmental noise across all nights to maintain data comparability.
review: Core tension: data completeness versus throughput; weakness: the options miss explicit strategies for balancing sensitivity and specificity in gating and handling missed events.

name: Collateral-informed recruitment and screening optimization
consequences: Direct effect: improves screen-to-enrollment efficiency and sample representativeness; downstream: increases upfront administrative workload and potential for selection bias if collateral data evolves over time.
options: Integrate collateral histories from bed partners and prior PSG reports into automated eligibility scoring to improve enrollment efficiency.
Pilot regionally targeted outreach through clinician referrals and campus screening days to raise enrollment yield without overburdening staff.
Introduce staged consent and pre-screening questionnaires to reduce dropouts before scheduling in Bonn and ensure eligibility alignment with study criteria.
review: Core tension: recruitment speed versus screening rigor; weakness: options miss potential ethical concerns or partner burnout.

name: EEG-BIDS data governance and annotation cadence
consequences: Direct effect: improved reliability and regulatory compliance for longitudinal data; downstream: increased governance overhead and documentation burden that may impact study velocity.
options: Institute a double-scoring workflow with independent raters and a fixed adjudication protocol to improve reliability.
Implement encrypted nightly backups and strict access controls to meet regulatory and privacy requirements across all data streams.
Standardize a shared data dictionary and audit trail to enable reproducible analyses and cross-study comparisons.
review: Core tension: reliability versus governance overhead; weakness: options miss how to handle versioning across annotation releases or cross-site interoperability.

name: Semi-automated event triage workflow with human adjudication
consequences: Direct effect: reduces manual workload and improves consistency in event adjudication; downstream: rigid criteria may miss nuanced events, potentially delaying rare episode confirmation.
options: Develop a rule-based ranking system that flags high-likelihood events for immediate human review while routing routine nights for batch scoring.
Define clear scoring criteria and thresholds to minimize ambiguous cases and maintain high inter-rater agreement across independent scorers.
Create a documentation protocol to trace decision paths and support reproducibility in longitudinal analyses across study phases.
review: Core tension: automation vs nuance; weakness: options miss fallback for unusual events or expert arbitration when criteria fail.

name: Capacity expansion governance with stage-gate decision framework
consequences: Direct effect: ensures expansion aligns with validated data quality and safety metrics; downstream: potential delays if milestones are not met, and financial planning becomes more conservative.
options: Define stage-gate expansion triggers based on enrollment pace and data throughput before increasing bed count.
Pilot a controlled expansion of two additional suites after achieving validated data quality and incident-free operation for a defined period.
Develop a flexible budget plan that models expansion scenarios against grant milestones and regulatory approvals to minimize financial risk.
review: Core tension: growth versus safety and data quality; weakness: options miss explicit contingency planning for external funding or partner-driven expansion.
